questions.question: Why should CSRF protection be disabled for stateless REST APIs that use JWT?
options: JWT tokens are immune to CSRF attacks by design
CSRF attacks exploit browser session cookies; JWT in Authorization headers are not sent automatically by browsers, so there's no CSRF risk
Spring Security's CSRF protection is incompatible with Jackson JSON serialization
Disabling CSRF is required for Spring Boot 3.x — it was removed from the default config

questions.question: What does SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS mean?
options: Spring Security will delete all existing sessions on startup
The application will not create or use HTTP sessions — each request must carry its own credentials
The application can only handle one user session at a time
Session data is stored in a stateless database instead of memory

questions.question: How does Spring Security interpret .roles("ADMIN") in UserDetails?
options: It creates a custom role named exactly 'ADMIN'
It grants the authority 'ROLE_ADMIN' — Spring automatically prepends 'ROLE_'
It requires the user to have a 'roles' field in the database with value 'ADMIN'
It is equivalent to .authorities("admin") — case is normalized automatically

questions.question: What is the purpose of BCryptPasswordEncoder's work factor (cost parameter)?
options: It controls the length of the generated hash
It sets how many characters are required in a valid password
It controls how slow the hashing is — higher values resist brute force by making each attempt more expensive
It determines how many rounds of salt are added to the password before storage

questions.question: Which annotation enables @PreAuthorize and @PostAuthorize on service methods?
options: @EnableSecurity
@EnableGlobalMethodSecurity (Spring Security 5) or @EnableMethodSecurity (Spring Security 6)
@Secured on the @Configuration class
@MethodSecurity on the @SpringBootApplication class
